返回本批次

衡量贫困的新方法显示美国落后于欧洲

A new way to measure poverty shows the US falling behind Europe

www.euronews.com 作者 _DeadFred_ 分数 88 评论 75 2026/04/02 01:02
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 比较经济和贫困具有挑战性，因为不同的措施可能会导致不同的结果。牛津大学经济学副教授奥利维尔·斯特克（Olivier Sterck）开发了一种衡量贫困的新方法，他称之为“平均贫困”。他发现“美国的平均贫困率要高得多，尽管平均收入高于大多数西欧国家”。当比较美国和欧洲的人均国内生产总值（GDP）时，数据显示出一个惊人的结果：美国最贫穷的州与德国不相上下。 2024 年第三季度，美国最贫穷的州密西西比州的人均 GDP 为 49,780 欧元（53,872 美元）。在德国，2024 年这一数字为 51,304 欧元，差距仅约为 1,500 欧元。正如《欧洲新闻商业》的一篇文章所示，以购买力平价（PPP）计算，美国的地位明显强于大多数欧盟国家（卢森堡和爱尔兰除外）。
  2. 什么是“平均贫困”？然而，奥利维尔·斯特克强调，将贫困视为一个范围会改变对话。它揭示了贫困线遗漏了什么以及不平等为何如此重要。根据斯特克在学术工作在线存储库 SSRN 上发表的研究，“平均贫困”被定义为赚取 1 美元所需的平均时间。 “该衡量标准具有包容性、对分配敏感、可分解，并且符合专家和公众对贫困的理解，”他说。 1 美元以国际元计算。这意味着它在任何国家购买的商品和服务的数量与美元在美国购买的商品和服务的数量相同。它通常与购买力平价 (PPP) 数据一起使用。 “时间”指的是任何人、任何年龄、任何情况下一天的生活——而不仅仅是有工作的人的工作时间。以国际美元赚取 1 美元所需的时间 截至 2025 年，在美国赚取 1 美元所需的时间为 63 分钟。这大约是德国、法国和英国平均水平的两倍。在欧洲最大经济体德国，则需要 26 分钟。在法国，这一数字为 31 分钟，而在英国则略有上升至 34 分钟。这些数字表明，美国的平均贫困率约为这三个国家的两倍。
  3. 使用这一指标，Sterck 发现自 1990 年以来全球贫困率下降了 55%。赚 1 美元所需的时间从大约半天减少到了 5 小时。美国的平均贫困率上升，欧洲的平均贫困率下降 新的衡量标准还显示，尽管平均收入增长强劲，但自 1990 年以来，美国的平均贫困率几乎持续上升。相比之下，大多数其他高收入国家的这一数字随着时间的推移而下降。例如，1990年，在美国赚1美元需要43分钟。这与法国（42 分钟）几乎相同，但比英国（51 分钟）短。德国队用时最短，为 34 分钟。 “从这些国家的人口中随机抽取两个人：他们的预期收入比在美国高于 4，但在三个欧洲国家仅为 1.5 左右。这表明美国的收入水平更加分散。因此，美国低收入个人的比例更高，他们需要更多的时间才能赚到 1 美元，”奥利维尔·斯特克告诉《欧洲商业新闻》。平均收入增长与平均不平等 根据这一指标，在过去 35 年里，美国赚取 1 美元所需的时间增加了 20 分钟，即 47%。欧洲三个经济体均出现下滑，其中英国跌幅最大。这是为什么？他指出，根据世界银行 PIP 数据，近几十年来，这四个国家的平均收入每年增长略高于 1%。然而，在美国，平均不平等每年增加约2.2%，超过了收入增长。 “这解释了美国平均贫困率上升的原因：平均不平等的增长速度快于平均收入的增长速度，”他说。相比之下，在英国、法国和德国，不平等仍然相对稳定，因此收入增长转化为平均贫困率的减少。增长中的经济体如何变得更穷“一个富裕国家的经济如何增长却变得更穷？”斯特克问道，他在《对话》的文章中提到了美国。他的答案很简单：不平等。他指出，贫困可能因两个主要原因而发生变化：收入增加或减少，或者收入分配变得或多或少不平等。就美国而言，即使在经济增长的情况下，平均贫困率也会增加，因为不平等的上升速度快于收入的增长速度。
  4. “美国是世界上最不平等的经济体之一，也是迄今为止富裕国家中最不平等的。自 1990 年以来，在所有 50 个州中，不平等现象急剧加剧，无论政治倾向、人口构成或经济结构如何，”他写道。以基尼系数衡量的收入不平等在美国高于欧洲主要经济体。值越高表示不平等程度越大。

Original Summary

  1. Comparing economies and poverty is challenging, as different measures can lead to different results. Olivier Sterck, an Associate Professor of Economics at the University of Oxford, has developed a new way to measure poverty, which he calls “average poverty”. He finds that “average poverty is substantially higher in the US, even though average incomes are higher than in most Western European countries”. When Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita is compared between the US and Europe, the figures suggest a striking result: the poorest US state rivals Germany. In the third quarter of 2024, Mississippi, the poorest US state, had a GDP per capita of €49,780 ($53,872). In Germany, it was €51,304 in 2024 — a gap of only about €1,500. In purchasing power parity (PPP) terms, the US is in a significantly stronger position than most EU countries, except for Luxembourg and Ireland, as a Euronews Business article shows.
  2. What is ‘average poverty’? However, Olivier Sterck emphasises that viewing poverty as a spectrum changes the conversation. It reveals what poverty lines miss and why inequality matters so much. According to Sterck’s research, published on SSRN, an online repository for academic work, “average poverty” is defined as the average time needed to earn $1. “The measure is inclusive, distribution-sensitive, decomposable, and aligns with how both experts and the public conceptualise poverty,” he says. The $1 is measured in international dollars. This means it buys the same amount of goods and services in any country as a US dollar does in the United States. It is often used alongside purchasing power parity (PPP) data. The “time” refers to a day of life for anyone, at any age and in any circumstance — not just the hours worked by someone with a job. Time needed to earn $1 in international dollars As of 2025, the time needed to earn $1 is 63 minutes in the US. This is about twice the average across Germany, France and the UK. In Germany, Europe’s largest economy, it takes 26 minutes. In France, the figure is 31 minutes, while in the UK it rises slightly to 34 minutes. These figures suggest that average poverty in the US is about twice that of these three countries.
  3. Using this metric, Sterck finds that global poverty has declined by 55% since 1990. The time needed to earn $1 has fallen from about half a day to five hours. Average poverty rises in the US, declines in Europe The new measure also shows that average poverty in the US has increased almost continuously since 1990, despite strong growth in average incomes. In contrast, it has declined over time in most other high-income countries. For example, in 1990, it took 43 minutes to earn $1 in the US. This was almost the same as in France (42 minutes) and shorter than in the UK (51 minutes). Germany had the lowest time at 34 minutes. “Take two individuals randomly from the populations of these countries: the expected ratio of their incomes is above 4 in the US, but only about 1.5 in the three European countries. This shows how income levels are much more dispersed in the US. As a result, there is a higher proportion of individuals with low incomes in the US, and they take more time to earn $1,” Olivier Sterck told Euronews Business. Growth in average income vs average inequality According to this metric, the time needed to earn $1 has risen by 20 minutes, or 47%, in the US over the past 35 years. All three European economies recorded declines, with the UK seeing the largest drop. Why is that? He points out that, in all four countries, average incomes have grown by a little over 1% per year over recent decades, according to World Bank PIP data. However, in the US, average inequality has increased by about 2.2% per year, outpacing income growth. “This explains why average poverty increased in the US: average inequality grew faster than average income,” he says. By contrast, in the UK, France and Germany, inequality remained relatively stable, so income growth translated into a reduction in average poverty. How growing economies become poorer “How can a rich country’s economy grow and yet become poorer?” Sterck asks, referring to the US in his article for The Conversation. His answer is simple: inequality. He notes that poverty can change for two main reasons: incomes rise or fall, or income distribution becomes more or less unequal. In the US case, average poverty increases even in a growing economy because inequality rises faster than incomes grow.
  4. “And the US has one of the most unequal economies in the world, and by far the most unequal among rich countries. Across all 50 states, inequality has risen sharply since 1990, regardless of political orientation, demographic composition or economic structure,” he writes. Income inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, is higher in the US than in major European economies. Higher values indicate greater inequality.

评论（默认中文）

Aurornis 2026/04/02 02:46
新措施：

> 截至 2025 年，在美国赚取 1 美元所需的时间为 63 分钟。

我很困惑，点击了其中一个链接并试图理解。发现这个：

> 获得 1 美元的时间是指任何年龄、任何情况下的任何人一天的生活，而不仅仅是有工作的人的工作时间。

单击另一个链接将我带到摘要处 https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4785458 但这也没有回答任何问题。

除了有人试图以令人困惑的方式重新定义很多术语之外，我在其中找不到任何真正实质性的内容

每 63 分钟 1 美元，即每年 8343 美元。我想不出任何方法可以将其与美国平均家庭收入或任何其他相关数字相一致。

The new measure:

> As of 2025, the time needed to earn $1 is 63 minutes in the US.

Confused, I clicked one of the links and tried to understand. Found this:

> The time to get $1 refers to a day of life for anyone at any age and in any circumstance, not just the hours worked by someone with a job.

Clicking another link took me to the abstract at https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4785458 but that didn't answer any questions either.

I can't find anything really of substance in this, other than someone trying to redefine a lot of terms in confusing ways

$1 every 63 minutes would be $8343/year. I cannot think of any way to reconcile that with the US average household income or any other related figure.

在 HN 中查看
laurencerowe 2026/04/02 08:17
我可以从 2017 年人口普查人员收入的所有数据表中得到一些接近的信息：

https://www.census.gov/data/tables/time-series/demo/income-p...

其中有很多人的收入处于 2500 美元区间。计算每个区间总和的区间内人数 * 1/(收入/每年分钟)，然后除以总收入，得到平均贫困时间为 49 分钟。

我认为他可能使用税后收入，并且可能根据家庭收入/家庭成员或类似的数据进行计算，这可以解释这种差异（因为孩子不工作）。

I can get somewhat close from the Census Person Income in 2017 all data tables:

https://www.census.gov/data/tables/time-series/demo/income-p...

That has numbers of people in $2500 income intervals. Calculate people in interval * 1/(income/minutes per year) for each interval sum and divide by total with income and I get an average poverty of 49 minutes.

I think he might be using after tax income and may be calculating based on household income/household members or similar instead which would explain the discrepancy (since children don't work).

在 HN 中查看
akamaka 2026/04/02 03:03
我也做了同样的计算。 我最接近的猜测是，它是根据一个四口之家的贫困线 32150 美元（除以四得到 8037 美元）得出的。
I did the same math. The closest guess I have is that it is derived from the poverty line for a family of four, $32150 (which divided by four is $8037).
在 HN 中查看
tovej 2026/04/02 03:08
这是因为它是每个人“赚 1 美元的时间”的平均值。

假设您是 Elon Musk，您花费的时间可以忽略不计，因此我们可以说 t_Elon = 0。

现在假设您远低于贫困线并且年收入 6000 美元。这意味着 t_Poor = 87 分钟。

如果我们对 80 t_Poor 和 20 t_Elon 进行平均，我们会发现我们得到 0.8 x 87 分钟 = 67 分钟。即使这种情况下的平均收入是 0.2 x Income_Elon。大约每年 70 亿美元。

我希望这能说明为什么你不能只取相反数来获得平均收入。唯一正确的方法是每个人都获得完全相同的收入。

为什么这是一个更好的指标？

平均收入偏向于高收入者，而这一指标则更多地围绕分配模式（穷人）。

它比平均收入更好地反映了收入分布。

That's because it is the average of the "time to earn 1$" per individual.

So let's say you're Elon Musk and it takes you a negligible enough time to do this that we can say that t_Elon = 0.

Now say you are way below the poverty line and earn 6000$/year. This means t_Poor = 87 mins.

If we average 80 t_Poor and 20 t_Elon we find we get 0.8 x 87 mins = 67 mins. Even when the average income in this case would be 0.2 x income_Elon. Something like 7 billion $/year.

I hope this shows why you can't just take the inverse to get the average income. The only way that was true was if everyone earned the exact same income.

Why is this a better metric?

The average income is biased towards big earners, while this metric is more centered around the mode of the distribution (poor people).

It captures the income distribution much better than average income.

在 HN 中查看
ffsm8 2026/04/02 03:34
嗯，平均值确实是一个毫无价值的指标，这就是为什么每个人都使用中位数来进行这些统计，除非他们是恶意争论的

如果你确实想使用平均值，你至少需要在计算之前从顶部和底部去除 10%，但它仍然是非常不可信的。

不知道从您的评论中删除什么，我仍然不确定您要提出什么样的指标以及为什么它是一个值得跟踪的有价值的东西

Well, Average is indeed a worthless metric, and that's why everyone is using median for these statistics unless they're arguing in bad faith

If you do want to use average, you'd at least need to remove 10% both from the top and bottom before calculating it, but it's still gonna be super untrustworthy.

Not sure what to take away from your comment, I'm still unsure what kind of metric you're pitching and why it'd be a valuable thing to track

在 HN 中查看
orangecat 2026/04/02 03:51
该指标更集中于分布模式（穷人）。

它的重点是最贫穷的人，他们不是时尚人士。 （收入分配近似对数正态；参见 https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-lognormal-distributi...）。

假设你有 10 个人：一个每年赚 800 美元，八个人每年赚 8 万美元，还有一个邪恶的亿万富翁每年赚 8 亿美元。他们赚取 1 美元的时间分别是 10 小时、0.1 小时和基本为零。如果计算算术平均值，则为 1.09 小时，而这一时间主要由单身穷人主导。如果你将该人的收入翻倍至 1600 美元，那么他们只需 5 小时即可赚取 1 美元，总体平均收入几乎减少一半，降至 0.58。与此同时，你可以将所有中产阶级的收入减少到 4 万美元，而且变化不大；达到 1 美元的平均时间为 (5+8(0.2)+0)/10=0.66。

它比平均收入更好地反映了收入分布。

事实并非如此，当然也不比人们通常使用的中等收入更好。它试图准确衡量最贫困人口的收入有多少，这通常不是人们谈论不平等或贫困时的意思，而且当微小的变化导致结果发生巨大波动时，也很难达到所需的准确性。特别是我不相信他正确地计算了政府福利；在美国几乎没有人的年消费低于 8000 美元。

this metric is more centered around the mode of the distribution (poor people).

It's focused on the very poorest, who are not the mode. (Income distribution is approximately lognormal; see https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-lognormal-distributi...).

Say you have 10 people: one making $800/year, 8 making $80k/year, and one evil billionaire making $800 million. Their times to earn $1 are respectively 10 hours, 0.1 hours, and essentially zero. If you take the arithmetic mean of that you get 1.09 hours, and that's dominated by the single poor person. If you double that person's income to $1600, then they're at 5 hours to earn $1, and the overall average is nearly cut in half to 0.58. Meanwhile you can reduce the income of all the middle class people to $40k and not much changes; the average time to $1 would be (5+8(0.2)+0)/10=0.66.

It captures the income distribution much better than average income.

Not really, and certainly not better than median income which is what people typically use. It tries to measure exactly how little income the very poor make, which is not normally what people mean when they talk about inequality or poverty, and also hard to measure at the accuracy that you need when small changes produce huge swings in the result. In particular I don't believe he's correctly accounted for government benefits; hardly anyone in the US is consuming less than $8000/year.

在 HN 中查看
megaman821 2026/04/02 04:09
感谢您的评论，我试图稍微解析一下“赚 1 美元所需的时间”的含义。这归结为国家为其最贫困成员提供的最高下限。
Thanks for the comment, I was trying to parse the meaning of "time needed to earn $1" for a bit. This just boils down to what countries have the highest floor for their poorest members.
在 HN 中查看
bee_rider 2026/04/02 02:22
我和其他人一样对美国经济的发展感到沮丧。但我们应该对一个具有直观名称的新指标持怀疑态度，它似乎准确地证实了我们所有人的怀疑，但解释/测量起来有点复杂，对吗？

尤其奇怪的是，只有我们在新冠疫情期间发生了巨大的变化。

德国总是比美国好，这似乎有点奇怪，即使是在 90 年代，当时这里的情况还不错。

总而言之，我们需要一直有新冠病毒在这里，这样我们才能在统一后立即赶上德国的经济发展。

I’m as frustrated as anybody else with how the economy is going in the US. But we should be skeptical about a new metric with an intuitive name that seems to confirm exactly what we all suspect but is sort of complex to interpret/measure, right?

In particular it seems weird that only we had a massive change during COVID.

Also seems a little odd that Germany was always better than the US, even in the 90’s when things were pretty good here.

Putting it together, we need to have COVID all the time here, so we can match the economic development of Germany immediately post-reunification.

在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 02:30
> 特别是，在新冠疫情期间只有我们发生了巨大的变化，这似乎很奇怪。

如果你当时已经足够大了，知道这个新闻，这并不奇怪。由于新冠疫情刺激计划，美国穷人突然发财并摆脱贫困，成为新闻周期的焦点。其他指出的国家没有遵循同样的“发放免费资金”的做法。他们的安全网是围绕在新冠疫情期间保持连续性而建立的。

> In particular it seems weird that only we had a massive change during COVID.

It is not weird if you were old enough to be aware of the news during that time. Poor people in the US suddenly coming into money and being lifted out of poverty thanks to COVID stimulus checks was front and center in the news cycle as it was happening. The other countries noted did not follow the same "hand out free money" approach. Their safety nets were built around maintaining continuity during COVID.

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/02 02:39
> 如果你当时年纪足够大，知道这个新闻，那并不奇怪。由于新冠疫情刺激计划，美国穷人突然发财并摆脱贫困，成为新闻周期的焦点。

关于刺激支票的文章已经写了很多，但它们规模太小，根本不重要。一张 1200 美元的支票不会突然让很多人摆脱贫困并让他们继续留在贫困中，尽管这对于某些选定的案例来说可能是成败的关键。

更大的变化是，美国经济基本上受到所有正在进行的干预措施的推动。还记得“大辞职”吗？当时每个人都在换工作，因为所有公司都在尽可能快地招聘员工？这是您在就业市场上取得更好职位的理想时机。

> It is not weird if you were old enough to be aware of the news during that time. Poor people in the US suddenly coming into money and being lifted out of poverty thanks to COVID stimulus checks was front and center in the news cycle as it was happening.

A lot was written about the stimulus checks but they were so small to not matter. A $1200 check isn't going to suddenly lift a lot of people out of poverty and keep them there, even though it could be make-or-break for a few selected cases.

The bigger change was that the American economy was basically turbocharged by all of the interventions going on. Remember "The Great Resignation" when everyone was changing jobs because all the companies were hiring as fast as they could? It was an ideal time to move your way into a better position in the job market.

在 HN 中查看
phainopepla2 2026/04/02 03:01
失业支票比刺激支票更能发挥作用。在新冠疫情期间，每周需要额外支付 600 美元。我认识很多人，他们在那段时间失业赚的钱比他们工作时赚的钱还要多。
It was the unemployment checks much more than the stimulus checks that made a difference. There was an extra $600/week tacked on to those during COVID. I know a lot of people who were making more on unemployment during that time than they ever had made while working.
在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 02:46
> 1200 美元的支票不会让很多人突然摆脱贫困

我认为“很多”是主观的。具体而言，它使 1170 万美国人[1] 摆脱了贫困。这约占刺激计划前贫困人口的 30%。

[1] https://www.commondreams.org/news/2021/09/14/incredible-covi...

> A $1200 check isn't going to suddenly lift a lot of people out of poverty

"A lot" is subjective, I suppose. Concretely, it lifted 11.7 million Americans[1] out of poverty. That makes up approximately 30% of those who were in poverty prior to the stimulus.

[1] https://www.commondreams.org/news/2021/09/14/incredible-covi...

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/02 02:53
那篇文章犯了我指出的同样的错误：它着眼于那段时间贫困的变化，并声称这一切都来自 1200 美元的刺激支票。

在那段时间里，很多事情都发生了变化，尤其是就业市场。找到一份每小时多付 1 美元的新工作比 1200 美元的刺激支票更有影响力。人们获得的加薪比这要大得多。

支票不是经济变化的主要驱动力

That article is just making the same mistake I pointed out: It looks at the change in poverty during that time and claims it all came from the $1200 stimulus checks.

A lot of things changed during that time, notably the job market. Getting a new job that paid $1/hour more would be more impactful than a $1200 stimulus check. People were getting raises much bigger than that.

The checks were not the primary driver of the economic changes

在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 02:57
> 那篇文章只是犯了我指出的同样的错误

这篇文章除了引用美国人口普查局的内容之外没有做任何其他事情。

显然，您已经阅读了全文，但对于其他人来说，这里是完整的引文：“作为与 COVID-19 大流行相关的经济救济立法的一部分而颁布的刺激付款，使 1170 万人摆脱了贫困。2020 年也扩大了失业保险福利，防止了 550 万人陷入贫困。”

同样，这是来自美国人口普查局。它是由一个有权访问所有相关数据的政府组织以官方政府身份提出的。如果你认为他们错了，你就必须提供比你当场胡编乱造的理论更令人信服的东西。

> That article is just making the same mistake I pointed out

The article doesn't do anything other than quote the US Census Bureau.

Obviously you will have already read the citation in full, but for everyone else here is the full quote: "Stimulus payments, enacted as part of economic relief legislation related to the COVID-19 pandemic, moved 11.7 million individuals out of poverty. Unemployment insurance benefits, also expanded during 2020, prevented 5.5 million individuals from falling into poverty."

Again, this is from the US Census Bureau. It is being asserted in an official government capacity, from an governmental organization that has access to all the relevant data. If you think that they got something wrong you're going to have to offer something more compelling than some random theory you made up on the spot.

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/02 03:00
我当时就经历过并讨论过它。我知道不同的政客和机构都在祝贺自己的刺激支票，因为在政治上，人们会与收到寄给他们的支票建立政治联系，但讨论整体经济则更为复杂。

这需要一点常识。一次性 1200 美元的刺激支票不会让 1170 万人从任何有意义的意义上摆脱贫困，除非你真的只是看着任意截止值 1200 美元以内的人，并说你通过在今年将他们提高到这个门槛来“让他们摆脱贫困”。

I lived through it and discussed it at the time. I'm aware of the the different politicians and agencies congratulating themselves for the stimulus checks because in politics people make political connections to receiving a check addressed to them but discussing the overall economy is more complex.

This one needs a little common sense. A one-time $1200 stimulus check is not going to lift 11.7 million individuals out of poverty in any meaningful sense, unless you're literally just looking at people within $1200 of an arbitrary cutoff and saying you "lifted them out of poverty" by bumping them over that threshold for the year.

在 HN 中查看
win311fwg 2026/04/02 03:08 deleted
[死的]
[dead]
在 HN 中查看
tpmoney 2026/04/02 05:31
即使我们从表面上看，1200个一次性支票使如此多的人摆脱了贫困，似乎也很难相信单次支票足以将这一“平均贫困”值从其他国家的2倍改变为不仅不到该值的一半，而且低于同一时期3个比较县中的2个，以及1990年美国被认为与可比国家同等的值的60%。从文章图表来看，没有一个比较国家在同一时期内看到其趋势出现哪怕是一丝一毫的变化。为了使这一点有意义，特别是考虑到本文的其余部分，似乎有一些事情必须是正确的：

1）美国财富不平等对美国贫困的所有影响可以通过每年给每个人一千美元来弥补。甚至 UBI 支持者也没有那么乐观。

2）在大规模的全球大流行期间，三个比较国家中的任何一个国家所做或没有做的任何事情都没有对其人口的相对贫困水平产生丝毫改变

3) 过去几十年的重大经济崩溃和衰退实际上改善了美国的平均贫困率（请注意，美国的贫困率在互联网泡沫破灭、9/11 事件和 2008 年金融危机开始时有所下降，尽管这些都没有得到政府的刺激支票。

这个测量可能有一些有趣的东西要说，但我不确定它是否表达了所声称的内容。感觉更像是他们发现了一种衡量美国经济和股市波动性更大的指标，而不是衡量贫困的指标。

Even if we take a face value the assertion that a 1200 one time check raised so many people out of poverty, it seems incredibly difficult to believe that single check for that single time was enough to change this "average poverty" value from 2x the other countries being referenced to not only less than half of that value, but also below 2 of the 3 comparative counties in the same time period AND 60% of the value in 1990 where the US was supposedly running equal with the comparative countries. Looking at the article graphs, none of the comparative countries see even a blip in their trends during the same time period. For that to make sense, especially in light of the rest of the article, it seems like a handful of things would have to be true:

1) That all of the effects of America's wealth inequality on American poverty could be made up for simply by giving everyone a thousand dollars a year. Not even UBI proponents are that optimistic.

2) That nothing any of the 3 comparative countries did or did not do during a massive global pandemic did anything to alter the relative poverty levels of their populations in the slightest

3) That the major economic crashes and recessions over the last few decades have actually improved American average poverty (notice that the US rate dips for the beginnings of the dot com crash, 9/11 and the 2008 financial crisis, despite none of those coming with government stimulus checks.

This measurement might be have something interesting to say, but I'm not sure it's saying what is being claimed. It feels more like they've found a more volatile measure of the US economy and stock market than of poverty.

在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 07:25
> 一张1200的一次性支票让这么多人摆脱了贫困

有一段时间每周 600 美元，再加上其他几项刺激计划，甚至不包括州级支持——总共约为 50,000 美元。也许如果你很富有，你只会看到一张相对较小的支票。但根据讨论，富有的人显然不是贫穷的人。

> 似乎很难相信单次的单次支票足以改变这个“平均贫困”值

相当。所以你承认你在准备评论时忽略了一些东西，但尽管如此，还是决定发布它？我可以看到要求澄清或帮助理解，但继续一些你已经完全意识到的长切线是没有意义的......？是什么激发了这种行为？

> that a 1200 one time check raised so many people out of poverty

It was $600 per week for a while, along with several other stimulus programs, not even including state-level support — totalling around $50,000 all told. Maybe if you were rich you only saw one relatively small check. But someone who was rich is obviously not someone who was in poverty, per the discussion.

> it seems incredibly difficult to believe that single check for that single time was enough to change this "average poverty" value

Quite. So you admit to recognizing that you overlooked something when preparing your comment but, despite that, decided to post it anyway? I could see asking for clarification or help in understanding, but going off on some long tangent that you already fully realize doesn't make sense...? What motivates that behavior?

在 HN 中查看
Balinares 2026/04/02 03:29
有趣的。我希望这一点能够流行起来——具体地描述贫困是很困难的，但评估人们平均需要多长时间才能获得给定的购买力单位是一个聪明的主意。意识到一个朋友需要花 100 多个小时才能筹集到一张我不会多看两眼的 100 美元钞票的资金，这让我发人深省……

尽管我希望这引发了一场关于我们如何做得更好而不是全国性的阴茎测量比赛的对话。这些没有帮助。

Interesting. I hope this catches on -- it's tough to visualize poverty in concrete terms, but assessing how long it takes a population to make a given unit of purchasing power in average is a clever idea. It's sobering to realize that it would take a friend across the sea over 100 hours to assemble the funds for a $100 bill that I wouldn't look at twice...

Although I wish this sparked a conversation on how we can do better instead of national dick measuring contests. Those don't help.

在 HN 中查看
laurencerowe 2026/04/02 02:37
平均值！=中位数。这一指标之所以如此之高，是因为美国由于最低工资较低而有大量低薪工人。

按购买力平价计算，美国工人的时薪中位数是发达国家中最高的，而且还在不断增加，但与德国的情况非常相似。 PPP 年薪的巨大差异在于工作时间。

2022 年，美国每名工人的平均年工作时间为 1790 小时，而德国为 1340 小时[1]。与此同时，美国 PPP 的平均时薪为 34.9 美元，而德国为 34.6 美元 [2]

[1] https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/annual-working-hours-per-...

[2] https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/average-hourly-earnings

Average != median. This measure seems to be so high because there are so many low paid workers in the US due to low minimum wage.

Median workers in the US have some of the highest hourly wages at PPP in the rich world and they have been increasing, but they are pretty similar to those in Germany. The big difference in annual pay at PPP is down to hours worked.

For 2022 average annual hours worked per worker in the US is 1790 while in Germany it is 1340 [1]. Meanwhile average hourly wages at PPP in US are $34.9 vs $34.6 in Germany [2]

[1] https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/annual-working-hours-per-...

[2] https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/average-hourly-earnings

在 HN 中查看
janalsncm 2026/04/02 02:53
我想知道由于将倒数相加，翻译是否也会出现一些困难，例如 1/(a+b+c+…) != 1/a+1/b+1/c+…
I wonder if there’s also some difficulty in translation happening due to adding up inverted numbers for example 1/(a+b+c+…) != 1/a+1/b+1/c+…
在 HN 中查看
orangecat 2026/04/02 03:57
这正是发生的事情。倒数对低端的微小变化非常敏感。
That's exactly what's going on. The inverses are very sensitive to small changes at the low end.
在 HN 中查看
tovej 2026/04/02 02:39
你忘记了，在美国，即使有 PPP，由于缺乏社会安全网，“舒适”水平也更高。

这意味着使用购买力平价实际上并不能显示不稳定的程度。

You forget that in the US, even with PPP, the level of "comfortable" is higher, due to missing social safety nets.

This means using PPP doesn't actually show where the level of precarity is.

在 HN 中查看
laurencerowe 2026/04/02 03:21
美国的生活肯定比欧洲更加不稳定，但这种情况已经持续了很长一段时间，而实际收入中位数在经历了 2001 年至 2015 年的停滞之后，此后一直在良好增长。

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/LES1252881600Q

感知和数据之间存在巨大的不匹配。我想知道某些成本是否更相关？

Life in the US is definitely more precarious than in Europe but that has been the case for a long time while median real earnings after stagnating from about 2001 to 2015 have been growing well since then.

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/LES1252881600Q

There is a huge mismatch between perception and data. I wonder whether some costs are just more pertinent?

在 HN 中查看
abighamb 2026/04/02 01:52
这个指标具有很大的直观意义，反映了我从邻居那里听到的消费者情绪。 “少花钱多干”并不是一个新的抱怨，但却是一个有趣的衡量标准。

我非常有兴趣了解这些统计数据与基尼系数或 GDP 之前的旧的原材料生产经济指标之间的关系。

This metric makes a lot of intuitive sense and reflects the consumer sentiment I hear from neighbors. "Working more for less" isn't a new complaint, but something that measures that is interesting.

I would be very interested to find out how those stats are related to things like, GINI or old pre-GDP economic measures of raw production.

在 HN 中查看
weberer 2026/04/02 02:02
这对我来说一点也不直观。从这篇文章中，我可以看到，在这个国家拥有更多的亿万富翁会带来某种惩罚，这会以某种方式导致“在美国赚 1 美元所需的时间是 63 分钟”，这与每小时最低工资从 7 美元到 18 美元不等（具体取决于州）的事实并不相符。

“旧”方法是衡量人均净购买力平价中位数，这对我来说更有意义：

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/85/Annual_m...

It is not intuitive at all to me. From the article, I can see that there's some sort of penalty by having more billionaires in the country, and that somehow leads to "the time needed to earn $1 is 63 minutes in the US", which doesn't really line up with the fact that minimum wage per hour ranges from $7-$18 depending on the state.

The "old" way was to measure median net PPP per capita, which makes more sense to me:

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/85/Annual_m...

在 HN 中查看
jltsiren 2026/04/02 02:32
您链接到的数字特别不直观。它显示每户收入除以家庭规模的平方根。像这样的启发法有很多复杂且不可预测的交互。例如，如果住房变得更便宜，年轻人可能会更快搬出去。那么低收入的小户就会增多，这可能会导致报告的收入下降。
The figure you linked to is particularly unintuitive. It shows income per household, divided by square root of household size. There are plenty of complex and unpredictable interactions with heuristics like that. For example, if housing becomes more affordable, young people may move out sooner. Then there will be more small households with low incomes, which may bring the reported income down.
在 HN 中查看
makapuf 2026/04/02 02:08
这个时间是全球平均水平，包括非工作人员或兼职人员。
This time is a global average, including non working people or part time people.
在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/04/02 02:44 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
codethief 2026/04/02 02:15
> 1 美元以国际元计算。这意味着它在任何国家购买的商品和服务的数量与美元在美国购买的商品和服务的数量相同。它通常与购买力平价 (PPP) 数据一起使用。 “时间”指的是任何人、任何年龄、任何情况下一天的生活——而不仅仅是有工作的人的工作时间。

那么IIUC这个“平均贫困”（以每国际美元的时间来衡量）包括靠社会福利生活的人？不然的话，如果只包括劳动人口，岂不是

  average poverty ≝ (average yearly income* of the working population / 1yr)⁻¹
所以它应该与文章中提到的平均年收入*指标成反比？

*) 根据购买力进行调整，即以国际美元衡量。

> The $1 is measured in international dollars. This means it buys the same amount of goods and services in any country as a US dollar does in the United States. It is often used alongside purchasing power parity (PPP) data. The “time” refers to a day of life for anyone, at any age and in any circumstance — not just the hours worked by someone with a job.

So IIUC this "average poverty" (measured in time per international dollar) includes people living off social welfare? Otherwise, if it only included the working population, wouldn't we have

  average poverty ≝ (average yearly income* of the working population / 1yr)⁻¹
and so it should be inversely proportional to the average yearly income* metric mentioned in the article?

*) Adjusted for purchasing power, i.e. measured in international dollars.

在 HN 中查看
runako 2026/04/02 02:17
来自链接的文章：

>出于这些目的，收入包括工作收入、政府福利和其他资金来源，并且是所有家庭成员的平均收入。

是的，它应该包括所有来源的收入。

https://theconversation.com/measuring-poverty-on-a-spectrum-...

From a linked article:

>For these purposes, income includes earnings from work, government benefits and other sources of money, and it is averaged among all family members.

Yes, it is supposed to include income from all sources.

https://theconversation.com/measuring-poverty-on-a-spectrum-...

在 HN 中查看
laurencerowe 2026/04/02 02:54
我认为这不太正确，它是时薪倒数的平均值，而不是平均时薪的倒数。

    >>> import statistics
    >>> 1/statistics.mean([10,30,100])
    0.02142857142857143
    >>> statistics.mean([1/10, 1/30, 1/100])
    0.04777777777777778
I don't think that's quite right, it would be the average of the inverse hourly wage not the inverse of the average hourly wage.

    >>> import statistics
    >>> 1/statistics.mean([10,30,100])
    0.02142857142857143
    >>> statistics.mean([1/10, 1/30, 1/100])
    0.04777777777777778
在 HN 中查看
mwhitfield 2026/04/02 02:41
不，是

平均贫困率≝平均（1/年收入）

反比于 调和平均数 的平均年收入。

No, it's

average poverty ≝ average(1 / annual income)

Inversely proportional to the harmonic mean of average yearly income.

在 HN 中查看
ktoyame 2026/04/02 01:58
美国的“平均贫困率”在新冠疫情中下降了 50% 以上，而欧洲则完全没有受到影响，这对我来说是违反直觉的。
it feels counterintuitive to me that US "average poverty" dropped more than 50 percent in covid, while european stayed absolutely untouched.
在 HN 中查看
graybeardhacker 2026/04/02 02:08
我也有同样的感觉。 但我认为原因类似于静坐会彻底破坏燃油经济性。 当你以零速度求平均值时，计算就会变得混乱。

美国人民已经如此接近金融灾难，为了避免灾难，美国不得不大量补贴那些失业的人。 许多人获得了原本无法获得的医疗保健和失业救济金。 这意味着零工时的收入。 当你平均为 1/0 小时时，它会对图表产生疯狂的影响。

现实情况是：新冠疫情期间，美国迅速采取了与欧盟国家类似的安全网，实际上与它们的贫困程度保持一致。 紧急措施结束后，我们又回到了之前不稳定的贫困水平。

只是我的理论。

I felt the same. But I think the reason is similar to how your fuel economy is absolutely destroyed by sitting still. When you average in a speed of zero the calculation goes haywire.

People in the US are so close to financial disaster that in order to avert disaster the US had to heavily subsidize those out of work. Many people got healthcare and unemployment benefits that would not have been otherwise available. This meant money for zero hours of work. When you average in $1/0 hours it does crazy things to the graph.

The reality is: During Covid the US rapidly adopted similar safety nets to EU countries and, in effect, aligned with their levels of poverty. Once the emergency measures ended we snapped back to our previous, precarious, poverty level.

Just my theory.

在 HN 中查看
unsnap_biceps 2026/04/02 02:04
与欧洲相比，其中有多少可能是由于美国缺乏真正的社交网络？

此外，有趣的是，我认识的在欧盟的每个在新冠疫情之前有工作的人今天都有工作。我不能对美国人说同样的话。

How much of that could be the lack of real social nets in the US compared to Europe?

In addition, anecdotally, everyone I know in the EU that had a job pre covid has a job today. I can't say the same thing about folks in the US.

makapuf 2026/04/02 02:06

The curious data point is that on this graph poverty seems to have strongly reduced during covid. Less poor.

milesskorpen 2026/04/02 02:11
新冠疫情期间，许多美国人收到了大量现金。
Huge amounts of cash were given to many Americans during Covid.

在 HN 中查看
jeffbee 2026/04/02 02:08
为什么这让你感到惊讶？最初是向儿童提供大量直接转移，然后是向所有人。这是美国有史以来规模最大、最有效的反贫困计划。
Why does this surprise you? There were large, direct transfers to initially children, then everyone. This was the largest and most effective American anti-poverty program of all time.
在 HN 中查看
weberer 2026/04/02 02:13
这不是我在芬兰的经历。几个月前失业率超过10%。目前青年失业率超过20%。
That's not my experience in Finland. The unemployment rate passed 10% a few months ago. Youth unemployment is now over 20%.
在 HN 中查看
tovej 2026/04/02 02:48
当然，这要归功于现任芬兰政府。削弱其福利体系，压低需求并造成激励陷阱。

IE。让经济更像美国。

Of course, this is due to the current Finnish govt. weakening its welfare system, driving down demand and creating incentive traps.

I.e. making the economy more like the US.

在 HN 中查看
weberer 2026/04/02 03:16
不，当前的紧缩措施是由于2023年（上次议会选举之前）的国家债务危机造成的。就连伊尔也事先承认了这一点。

https://yle.fi/a/74-20007883

https://yle.fi/a/74-20017437

No, the current austerity measures are due to the national debt crisis in 2023, before the last parliamentary election. Even Yle acknowledged this beforehand.

https://yle.fi/a/74-20007883

https://yle.fi/a/74-20017437

在 HN 中查看
tovej 2026/04/02 03:18
我什至没有提及紧缩措施的原因，我只是描述了它们的效果。

你甚至没有回复我帖子中的任何内容。请再试一次。

I did not even mention the causes of austerity measures, I'm simply describing their effect.

You are not even responding to anything in my post. Please try again.

在 HN 中查看
weberer 2026/04/02 03:22
你指责“现任”芬兰政府采取紧缩措施，并含蓄地消除了上届政府首先推高债务的责任。我只会责怪“芬兰政府”（所有政府）。
You're blaming the "current" Finnish government for austerity measures and implicitly removing blame from the previous administration for driving up the debt in the first place. I would just blame "the Finnish government" (over all administrations).
在 HN 中查看
mikkupikku 2026/04/02 02:08
政客们是根据这些指标来评判的，所以他们都会被欺骗。
Politicians are judged by these metrics, so they all get gamed.
在 HN 中查看
jojomodding 2026/04/02 02:10
可悲的是，他们不是。或者更确切地说，许多选民关心许多不同的事情，而由此产生的指标并不那么明智。
They are not, sadly. Or rather, many voters care about many different things and the resulting metric is not that sensible.
在 HN 中查看
jeffbee 2026/04/02 02:09
这根本不违反直觉，除非你误解了穷人的收入水平。向每个人发放 1400 美元，极大地增加了最贫困美国人的收入。
It is not counterintuitive at all, unless you misunderstand the income levels of the poor. Sending everyone $1400 massively increased the income of the poorest Americans.
在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 02:07
对 COIVD 关闭的不同反应。美国政府直接向人民提供刺激资金，为许多人带来了在此期间收入的增加。欧洲的应对措施更侧重于帮助人们保住工作，从而保持收入稳定。
Different responses to the COIVD shutdowns. The US government gave stimulus money directly to the people, for many bringing an increase to one's income during that period. The European response was focused more on helping people keep their jobs so their incomes remained stable.
在 HN 中查看
mikkupikku 2026/04/02 02:10
非常怀疑刺激措施是否足以有效降低实际贫困水平。 那些已经靠工资过活的人几乎一拿到工资就花掉了（希望能花在一些能有意义地改善他们生活的事情上，比如推迟汽车或房屋维修，但如果我们说实话，很多人都在赌博应用程序上浪费了钱。）
Extremely doubtful that the stimmy was enough to meaningfully reduce the real poverty level. The people who already live paycheck to paycheck spent that almost as soon as they got it (hopefully on something that meaningfully improved their lives, like deferred car or home repairs, but if we're being real a lot of people blew it on gambling apps.)
在 HN 中查看
9rx 2026/04/02 02:11
该指标衡量的是获得 1 美元收入需要多长时间。在一段时间内，当你的收入增加时（在这种情况下，因为政府开始支付给你的钱比你以前得到的多），获得 1 美元的时间就会减少。当政府停止支付并且你回到原来的状态时，获得 1 美元的时间就会恢复。
The metric in question measures how much time it takes to get $1 in income. When, over a set period of time, your income increases (in this case because the government started paying you more than you were getting before), the time to get $1 goes down. When the government stops paying and you go back to the way things were then the time to get $1 goes back up.
在 HN 中查看
jeffbee 2026/04/02 02:13

https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/federal-tax-credits-i...

Incorrect.

https://www.nationalacademies.org/news/federal-tax-credits-i...

在 HN 中查看
j_french 2026/04/02 01:56
本文引用了《The Conversation》中 Sterck 的文章： https://theconversation.com/measuring-poverty-on-a-spectrum-...
Sterck's article from The Conversation referenced in this article: https://theconversation.com/measuring-poverty-on-a-spectrum-...
在 HN 中查看
WarmWash 2026/04/02 02:18
我猜这是因为像德国这样的地方每年的工作时间非常低。[1]列表底部是所有欧洲国家。

[1]https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_average_a...

I would guess this is because places like Germany having incredibly low annual working hours.[1] The bottom of the list is populated by all European countries.

[1]https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_average_a...

在 HN 中查看
adrian_b 2026/04/02 02:33
位于该列表的底部是非常理想的，因为这意味着高质量的生活，而位于该列表的顶部则意味着生活质量非常低。

提高工作生产率的目标必须是通过减少工作量来生产相同的产品，而不是为了为社会中微不足道的一部分人赚取更高的利润而进行相同的工作。

Being at the bottom of that list is very desirable, because it means high quality of life, while being at the top of that list means very low quality of life.

The goal of increasing work productivity must be to produce the same by working less, not to work the same in order to make higher profits for a negligible part of the society.

在 HN 中查看
FeloniousHam 2026/04/02 03:38
这听起来很像欧洲电影： https://www.noahpinion.blog/p/eurocope
This sounds like so much Eurocope: https://www.noahpinion.blog/p/eurocope
在 HN 中查看
heathrow83829 2026/04/02 03:07
这篇文章没有解释数学是如何运作的。 如果最低工资是每小时 15 美元或 10 美元，那么他们如何达到 63 分钟的 1 美元？？？
the article doesn't explain how the math works. if min wage is 15$ an hour or 10$, how do they arrive at 1$ for 63 min???
在 HN 中查看
tovej 2026/04/02 03:16
请参阅我在该线程其他地方的评论： https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47605141
See my comment elsewhere in this thread: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47605141
在 HN 中查看
joe_the_user 2026/04/02 02:58
他发现“美国的平均贫困率要高得多，尽管平均收入高于大多数西欧国家”。

这似乎是一种“谈论中等收入而不谈论中等收入”的复杂方式。到最后，他们确实描述了一个基本情况：美国的总财富和总收入更大，但财富和收入分配不均，导致更多的人陷入贫困。

He finds that “average poverty is substantially higher in the US, even though average incomes are higher than in most Western European countries”.

That seems like a complicated way to "talk about median income without talking about median income". By the end, they do describe the basic situation: US has greater total wealth and total income but that wealth and income is so unequally distributed that more people are poor.

在 HN 中查看
Ekaros 2026/04/02 03:18
生活成本和商品成本在外面听起来很荒谬。比如租金和汽车付款以及保险。甚至像一些餐馆之类的东西...... Ofc，外面有昂贵的地方，但也有很多更合理的地方。从外部来看，美国在成本方面存在一些问题。
Cost of living and cost of goods sounds ludicrous from outside. Like rents and car payments, and insurance. And even stuff like some restaurants... Ofc, there is expensive locations outside but there is also lot more reasonable places. From outside it feels that there is something especially broken in cost wise in USA.
在 HN 中查看
AnimalMuppet 2026/04/02 02:21
好吧，这个想法很有趣，但这些数字似乎完全是假的。 在什么样的世界里，美国人平均需要 63 分钟才能赚到 1 美元，甚至是 1 美元“国际”美元？

我得到了“国际”部分——购买力。 不过，这个数字似乎仍然相差甚远。

在最低工资为 7 美元/小时的时代，美国人的平均收入是 1 美元/小时吗？

谁能让这个数字有意义吗？

OK, the idea is interesting, but the numbers seem completely bogus. In what world does it take the average American 63 minutes to earn $1, even one "international" dollar?

I get the "international" part - purchasing power. The number still seems way off, though.

In a time when minimum wage is $7/hr, how is the average American earning $1/hr?

Can anyone make that number make any sense?

在 HN 中查看
akamaka 2026/04/02 02:30
不，我只花了 15 分钟阅读原始论文，无法理解他在计算什么。

国际美元已标准化为美元，因此无需转换。 他引用的数字是每美元 63 分钟，换算成每年 8343 美元。 然而，他的原始论文指出，他是通过倒转收入来创建这一衡量标准的，因此数字8343是他的起点。

我最接近的猜测是根据一个四口之家的贫困线得出的，即 32150 美元（除以四即为 8037 美元）。

如果是这样的话，他真正做的就是比较各国之间的贫困线定义。

Nope, I just spent 15 minutes reading the original paper and can’t make any sense of what he is calculating.

International dollars are normalized to USD, so there’s no conversion necessary. The figure he quotes of 63 min per dollar converts to $8343/year. However, his original paper states that he created this measure by inverting income, so the number 8343 is his starting point.

The closest guess I have is that is derived from the poverty line for a family of four, $32150 (which divided by four is $8037).

If that is the case, what he is really doing is comparing poverty line definitions between countries.

在 HN 中查看
hnthrow0287345 2026/04/02 02:29
>“时间”是指任何人、任何年龄、任何情况下一天的生活——而不仅仅是有工作的人的工作时间。

所以就是你每天的收入除以 24，或者除以年收入和每年的工作时间

>The “time” refers to a day of life for anyone, at any age and in any circumstance — not just the hours worked by someone with a job.

So it's how much you earn per day divided by 24, or maybe by yearly earnings and hours per year

在 HN 中查看
rassimmoc 2026/04/02 02:40
您无法将文章中的计算与最低工资进行比较。

这样想，就像中位数收入和平均收入之间的差异。不平等越大，中位数和平均数之间的差距就越大。

You can't compare calculations in article with minimum wage.

Think of it this way, its like difference between median and average income. Larger inequality, larger the gap between median and average.

在 HN 中查看
IshKebab 2026/04/02 02:13
这似乎是衡量贫困的一个很好的方法。
This seems like a quite nice way to measure poverty.
在 HN 中查看
wilg 2026/04/02 02:11
我相信这就是纸 https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid%3A501e8eb8-3ce7-4ac0-9d09-...
I believe this is the paper https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid%3A501e8eb8-3ce7-4ac0-9d09-...
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/04/02 02:16
精彩的介绍，快速解释了拟议新措施的理由：

> 几乎每个人都会同意，20 米高的树是 10 米树高的两倍。相反，每个人都会同意 10 米树比 20 米树短两倍。不存在高于或低于这些关系的阈值或“短线”：5 米树的长度是 10 米树的两倍，1 米树的长度是 2 米树的两倍，等等。当考虑其他倍数时，这一推理仍然有效：1 米树比 3 米树短三倍。可以肯定的是，在评估一棵树的高度时，不同的人可能会不同意它是矮还是高，因为他们的判断将取决于他们评估时使用的基准。然而，当比较两棵不同的树时，几乎每个人都会进行类似的基本比较。用数学术语来说，矮是身高的倒数。 [...]在本文中，我应用相同的逻辑来定义新的贫困衡量标准

Great intro that quickly explains the reasoning for the proposed new measure:

> Virtually everyone would agree that a 20-meter tree is twice as tall as a 10-meter tree. Conversely, everyone would agree that the 10-meter tree is twice as short as the 20-meter tree. There is no threshold or “shortness line” above or under which these relationships cease to hold: a 5-meter tree is twice as short as a 10-meter tree, a 1-meter tree is twice as short as a 2-meter tree, and so on. This reasoning remains valid when considering other multiples: a 1-meter tree is three times shorter than a 3-meter tree. To be sure, when assessing the height of a single tree, different people may disagree whether it is short or tall, as their judgment will depend on the benchmark they use for their assessment. However, when comparing two different trees, virtually everyone would make similar cardinal comparisons. In mathematical terms, shortness is the reciprocal of tallness. [...] In this paper, I apply the same logic to define a new poverty measure

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/02 02:49
有很多词语与所声称的措施无关。

我仍在试图弄清楚他是如何得出在美国需要 63 分钟才能赚到 1 美元的结论的

There are a lot of words that have nothing to do with the claimed measure.

I'm still trying to figure out how he reached the conclusion that it takes 63 minutes to earn $1 in the US

在 HN 中查看
orangecat 2026/04/02 04:03
这很愚蠢。从任何有意义的意义上来说，年收入 100 美元的人并不比年收入 200 美元的人“贫穷两倍”；两者都极其贫困，并且需要基本相同数量的公共支持。但这一指标将差异视为如此巨大（80 小时赚取 1 美元与 40 小时赚取 1 美元），以至于掩盖了收入分配其他方面的任何差异。
And it's silly. A person earning $100 a year is not "twice as poor" as a person earning $200 in any meaningful sense; both are extremely poor and will require essentially the same amount of public support. But this metric treats the difference as so huge (80 hours to earn $1 vs 40) that it drowns out any differences in the rest of the income distribution.
在 HN 中查看
charcircuit 2026/04/02 02:58
忽视房价和股市等因素似乎有失偏颇，近年来我们在这些方面看到了一些巨大的涨幅。如果投资房地产、公司、债券、国债或其他任何东西来赚钱很容易。这应该算。
It seems biased to ignore things like growth in housing prices and the stock market where we have seen some massive gains in recent years. If it's easy to invest in property or companies or bonds or treasuries or whatever to make a dollar. That should count.
在 HN 中查看
josefritzishere 2026/04/02 03:18
美国生活水平的下降已经变得非常明显。我认为我们的解决方案已经过去了。问题是它会走意大利的路还是南斯拉夫的路。
The declining standard of living in the USA is has become painfully obvious. I think we're past solutions. The question is if it will go the way of Italy or the way of Yugoslavia.
在 HN 中查看
rexpop 2026/04/02 04:46
在美国谈论贫困而不谈论种族是没有意义的（或者，好吧，对于观众中的自由主义者来说：“地理”）。这当然没有意义 平均的 人口的经济地位分为帝国核心和殖民地飞地。
It doesn't make sense to talk about poverty in the US without talking about race (or, ok, for you libertarians in the audience: "geography"). It certainly doesn't make sense to average the economic standing of a population split between imperial core and colonized enclaves.

wyre 2026/04/02 01:56

PaulKeeble 2026/04/02 02:35
他们使用的通胀指标是否会随着时间的推移进行调整？如果不是这样，这表明随着时间的推移，普通人的购买能力会出现巨大损失，这肯定是过去几十年的感受，因为通货膨胀已经超过了大多数人的工资。
Is the measure they are using inflation adjusted over time? If not this shows an enormous loss in purchasing capacity over time for the average person, which is certainly how its felt over the past decades as inflation has outrun wages for most people.
在 HN 中查看
